text: This is a video of single incision distal gastroctomy with delta anastomosis.
The surgeon begins the gastrectomy procedure by making an incision for the single incision port, preparing the surgical site for access.
The surgeon makes a skin incision measuring 2.5 to 4 centimeters and inserts the glove port into the incision site.
The surgeon inserts a needle passer with a suture below the sternum, penetrates the falciform ligament, and retrieves the suture, pulling it outside the body.
The surgeon opens the lesser sac and fixes the suture to the gastrophrenic ligament, securing it in place.
The surgeon places a gauze pad between the suture and the liver to prevent hepatic injury during the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs a partial omentectomy, removing a portion of the omentum from the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the lesser sac, revealing the posterior wall of the stomach, which is clearly visualized within the surgical field.
The surgeon clears the greater curvature of the stomach just above the predicted resection site, preparing the area for further surgical steps.
The surgeon uses a bipolar energy device to ligate the left gastroepiploic vessels, visibly securing them to prevent bleeding.
The surgeon performs an omentectomy on the right side, carefully excising the omental tissue adjacent to the stomach.
The surgeon carefully dissects the right side omentum, taking care to avoid injury to the adjacent colon, with the omentum visibly separated from surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies the duodenum and dissects the number six lymph node area around the right gastroepiploic vessels, carefully separating the lymphatic tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies the pancreas head and dissects the right gastroepiploic vessels located above the pancreas, ligating them with precision.
The surgeon clears the greater curvature of the stomach, ensuring adequate space for the upcoming delta anastomosis procedure.
A gauze is inserted beneath the dissected pylorus to facilitate safe dissection, providing a clear view and protecting surrounding tissues during the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs dissection along the lesser curvature of the stomach, ensuring adequate clearance for the upcoming delta anastomosis.
The surgeon identifies and ligates the right gastric artery. The pylorus is rotated ninety degrees to establish a transverse resection line, facilitating the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a linear stapler to transect the distal end of the stomach, completing the resection with precise alignment.
The surgeon begins suprapancreatic lymph node dissection, focusing on the number eight lymph node located around the common hepatic artery.

The surgeon performs lymph node dissection along the upper border of the pancreas and around the celiac artery, observing enlarged lymph nodes. The dissection continues in the number eight lymph node area and above the common hepatic artery.
The surgeon dissects and ligates the coronary vein, followed by the dissection and ligation of the left gastric artery, ensuring clear visualization of these vascular structures.
The surgeon continues clearing the lesser curvature of the stomach, removing surrounding tissue to expose the anatomical structure.
The surgeon ligates the anterior vagus nerve and proceeds with dissection of the number three lymph node area, carefully separating the lymphatic tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon transacts the stomach at the pre-marked site, visibly separating the stomach tissue.

The surgeon pulls the proximal stomach towards the duodenal stump to ensure there is no tension. A hole is created on the greater curvature of the proximal stomach, and another hole is made on the medial side of the duodenal stump. Sutures are placed on both the duodenal stump and the proximal stomach for traction purposes.

The surgeon inserts the 45mm cartridge side of the linear stapler into the hole on the proximal stomach, maintaining tension on the suture to prevent slippage. The stapler and stomach are pulled towards the duodenal stump, where the anvil side of the stapler is inserted into the hole on the duodenal stump. Both the proximal stomach and duodenal stump are everted, allowing their posterior surfaces to meet, and the stapler is fired to join them.

The surgeon places a tagging suture on the superior anastomosis line and another tagging suture on the posterior anastomosis line, securing the newly formed connection between the proximal stomach and duodenal stump.
The surgeon uses a linear stapler with a sixty millimeter cartridge to close the entry hole, completing the delta anastomosis between the proximal stomach and duodenal stump.
